title: dict: the lock-free FT dict iterator reads ma_values non-atomically vs set_values' atomic publish (dictiter_iternext_threadsafe:6043)
description: dictiter_iternext_threadsafe (the free-threaded lock-free dict-iterator next) tests _PyDict_HasSplitTable(d) at dictobject.c:6043, a macro that plainly reads d->ma_values. A concurrent setattr() that overflows an instance's full shared keys converts its __dict__ split->combined in dictresize, publishing the new ma_values (NULL) via set_values -> _Py_atomic_store_ptr_release. Plain read vs atomic store on the same word is a data race -- and the very next line (6044) already reads ma_values atomically with _Py_atomic_load_ptr_consume, so this is an incomplete atomic conversion, not the general 'don't share a dict' class.
sites: Objects/dictobject.c dictiter_iternext_threadsafe:6043 (PLAIN read of ma_values via _PyDict_HasSplitTable(d) == ((d)->ma_values != NULL))
Objects/dictobject.c dictiter_iternext_threadsafe:6044 (adjacent ATOMIC read of the SAME field: _Py_atomic_load_ptr_consume(&d->ma_values) -- proves the conversion is incomplete)
Objects/dictobject.c dictresize:2222 (set_values(mp, NULL) on split->combined conversion, oldvalues != NULL)
race_kind: incomplete atomic conversion (residual plain read vs atomic writer) in the lock-free FT dict iterator
shared_state: PyDictObject.ma_values (split-table value-array pointer, published atomically by set_values, nulled on split->combined dictresize)
severity: low (value-benign, crash-free; aligned-pointer read re-validated atomically one line later at :6044; QSBR retires the old values array so it is not a UAF) -- but a genuine data race in the routine that is explicitly the thread-safe lock-free iterator
repro_reliability: deterministic (~0.7s, exit 66); fresh full-shared-keys split instance each round so the first setattr forces the split->combined set_values(NULL) while iterators re-check HasSplitTable
suggested_fix: hoist the atomic consume-load already on :6044 above the split-table test and branch on it (replace the plain _PyDict_HasSplitTable(d) at :6043 with a single _Py_atomic_load_ptr_consume(&d->ma_values)); or make _PyDict_HasSplitTable atomic under Py_GIL_DISABLED (heavier, ~15 call sites, most lock-held)
confirmed_unfixed_on: heads/main:bcf98ddbc40 (Jul 2026): Include/internal/pycore_dict.h:56 _PyDict_HasSplitTable is still a plain ((d)->ma_values != NULL) read, used lock-free at dictobject.c:6043
ruling: Same class as Thomas Wouters (Yhg1s, CPython RM) 2026-07-15 ruling ('non-atomic reader should use the atomic accessor the writer already uses'); distinct dict-specific instance and a clearer one -- the adjacent line 6044 already does the atomic read, so it is an incomplete conversion inside the deliberately-lock-free FT iterator, not the 'don't share this object' bucket.
assessment: Real, NEW, clean incomplete-atomic-conversion: dictiter_iternext_threadsafe:6043 plain-reads d->ma_values (_PyDict_HasSplitTable) that dictresize publishes atomically -- and the NEXT LINE 6043->6044 already reads it atomically (_Py_atomic_load_ptr_consume). Fits the Yhg1s shared-container ruling; same class as TSAN-0013, distinct dict instance. Fix: one line. Worth umbrella. Neighbor residual: ma_used at get_index_from_order:674.

related_issues.note: CLOSED as DUPLICATE: 'data race in dictiter_iternext with free-threading' -- reads di->di_dict while written. Same dict-iterator-under-FT class (siblings: #126366, #131113, both closed). Our finding is a narrower incomplete-atomic-conversion (dictiter_iternext_threadsafe:6043 plain-reads ma_values that the very next line :6044 reads atomically), a different field than #148873's di_dict, but the same known area -- likely a residual, lower confidence as a standalone NEW.

notes: API-SEARCH 2026-07-17: the dict-iterator-under-FT class is KNOWN (#148873 closed-as-dup, #126366, #131113). This is a narrow residual (one plain load next to an atomic one), not a novel class. gh api only. Fleet-06 (2026-07-18) added the shared-cursor face `_Py_atomic_load_ssize_relaxed | dictiter_iternextitem_lock_held`: two threads advancing ONE shared dict iterator race in the iternext_threadsafe machinery (lock-free size read at :6082 vs the lock_held path at :6017) -- the dict analog of the bytes/str shared-iterator cursor race (TSAN-0037 / cpython#153928), distinct mechanism from this entry's ma_values face but same dictiter_iternext_threadsafe home.
